unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 6! In this unit you will explore the tiny world of bugs, insects, and other small garden animals. You will learn the names of butterflies, bees, ants, spiders and more, and discover what makes an insect special — six legs, three body parts, and antennae. You will learn where these little creatures live and what they do. You will also learn position words like on, under and between to say exactly where a bug is. By the end, you will be able to describe a bug of your own, just like Samira does in this unit's story.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher audiovisual lecture and learn a few vocabulary words each day.
Complete all six grammar practices, moving from guided examples to independent use.
Listen to the Unit readings, including Samira and the Little Garden Friends, and answer the comprehension questions.
Complete all six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question quiz and student self-assessment.

readings.title: What Is an Insect?
readings.passageScript:
Read this short text:
Insects are small animals with special features. All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen. Most insects have two antennae on their heads. Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them. Many insects have wings, but not all of them. Butterflies, bees, and crickets are all insects. Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all. There are more kinds of insects in the world than any other type of animal!

readings.title: A Bug Poem
readings.passageScript:
Read this short poem, then try to say it from memory:
One, two, three!
There's a bug on me.
Where did it go?
I don't know.

readings.title: The Grandpa's Cricket
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Selma talk with her grandpa. (Ask your AI tutor to read it aloud, then read it yourself.)
“Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” — “It is a cricket. I found it in the garden.” “Can I keep it as a pet? I love the chirping sound!” — “If you keep a cricket, you must take good care of it. Crickets need to stay warm. They eat small pieces of fruit and vegetables, and they need a little dish of water.”

readings.title: Samira and the Little Garden Friends
readings.theme: Being curious about bugs, caring for small creatures, and not being afraid.
readings.setting: A family garden in a Somali town.
readings.passageScript:
Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house. The sun was soft and gold. Birds sang from the acacia tree, and the air smelled of wet soil after the night's rain.
Samira loved the garden. But there was one thing she did not love: bugs. When a bee buzzed near her, she ran. When she saw a spider, she covered her eyes.
Her older brother Ishmael was already there, sitting very still on a low rock.
“Come, Samira,” he whispered. “Sit with me. If you look closely, the garden is full of little friends.”
Samira sat down slowly. On a bright red flower, a butterfly opened and closed its wings.
“Look,” said Ishmael. “A butterfly is an insect. It has six legs, and two antennae on its head. Its antennae help it feel and smell. It drinks sweet nectar from the flower.”
The butterfly did not seem scary at all. Samira smiled a little.
Then a bee flew above the flowers, going from one to the next.
“Is the bee going to sting me?” Samira asked, pulling back.
“No,” said Ishmael gently. “The bee is very busy. It is collecting nectar to make honey. If you stay calm and do not wave your arms, the bee will leave you alone.”
Samira held still. The bee buzzed near her hand, then flew away. She let out a happy breath. She was not afraid now.
Near her foot, a long line of ants marched over the soil. They were carrying tiny seeds.
“Where are they going?” Samira asked.
“To their home,” said Ishmael. “Ants live in a big anthill under the ground. They work together and share everything. One ant is small, but many ants are very strong.”
Samira watched the ants for a long time. She liked how they helped each other, just like a big family.
Then she saw something between two branches of the small lemon tree. It was a web, shining with drops of water. In the middle sat a spider.
Samira wanted to run, but she did not.
“A spider is not an insect,” said Ishmael. “Count its legs.”
Samira counted, one by one. “Eight legs!” she said. “Insects have only six.”
“That is right,” said Ishmael, proud of her. “A spider spins its web from silk in its own body. The web catches insects for the spider to eat. It is not here to hurt you. It is only doing its work.”
Samira looked at the web. It was beautiful, like tiny threads of glass.
Just then, their little sister Amal came running. She lifted her foot to stamp on a fat worm on the path.
“Wait!” called Samira. “Do not step on it. A worm has no legs, but it is a good little animal. Worms crawl through the soil and help our plants grow strong.”
Selma stopped. Together, the three children watched the worm wriggle safely under a leaf.
That evening, Samira told her mother everything. “I used to be afraid of bugs,” she said. “But now I know they are little friends. The bee makes honey, the ants work together, the spider spins a web, and the worm helps the soil.”
Her mother smiled. “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it. And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.”
Samira looked out at the dark garden, where all her little friends were resting. She could not wait for morning, to sit on the rock and watch them again.

comprehension.question: How many legs do all insects have? How many body parts?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen.

comprehension.question: What do antennae help insects do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them.

comprehension.question: Why is a spider not an insect? Why is a worm not an insect?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all.

comprehension.question: What is the poem about?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Where did it go?
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Where did it go?

comprehension.question: What happened to the bug at the end?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Where did it go?
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Where did it go?

comprehension.question: Can you make your own short poem about a bug?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

comprehension.question: What was in Grandpa's pocket? Where did he find it?
comprehension.correctAnswer: A cricket was in Grandpa's pocket, and he found it in the garden.
comprehension.explanation: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

comprehension.question: Why does Selma want a cricket as a pet?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” — “It is a cricket.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” — “It is a cricket.

comprehension.question: What does a pet cricket need to stay healthy?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Crickets need to stay warm.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Crickets need to stay warm.

comprehension.question: At the start of the story, how did Samira feel about bugs?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.

comprehension.question: What did Ishmael say a butterfly uses its antennae for?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Her older brother Ishmael was already there, sitting very still on a low rock.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Her older brother Ishmael was already there, sitting very still on a low rock.

comprehension.question: Why did the bee not sting Samira?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Is the bee going to sting me?” Samira asked, pulling back.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Is the bee going to sting me?” Samira asked, pulling back.

comprehension.question: How did Samira know the spider was not an insect?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “A spider is not an insect,” said Ishmael.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “A spider is not an insect,” said Ishmael.

comprehension.question: Why did Samira stop Amal from stepping on the worm?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.

comprehension.question: How did Samira change from the beginning of the story to the end? What helped her change?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house. Samira loved the garden.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house. Samira loved the garden.

comprehension.question: Samira's mother said, “When we understand something, we stop being afraid of it.” Is there something you were afraid of, but are not afraid of now?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it.

comprehension.question: How can we care for small creatures in our own gardens and homes?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.” Samira looked out at the dark garden, where all her little friends were resting.
comprehension.explanation: The response should point to this evidence from the text: And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.” Samira looked out at the dark garden, where all her little friends were resting.

grammar.title: Position Words
grammar.explanation: Position words tell us where something is. When we watch bugs, we need these words to say exactly where each one is — on a leaf, under a rock, or in a hole.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
on → touching the top:  “The butterfly is on the flower.”
under → below:  “The worm is under the rock.”
in → inside:  “The ant is in the anthill.”
between → in the middle of two things:  “The cricket is between two leaves.”
above → higher than:  “The bee is above the flower.”
in front of → just before:  “The spider is in front of its web.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The ant is on the anthill.” (when it is inside)  ✗
Fix: If the ant is inside, we say in: “The ant is in the anthill.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Use on when something touches the top of a thing. Use in when something is inside a thing. A bug on a box sits on top; a bug in a box is inside it.
grammar.practice:
Fill in the best position word (on, under, in, between, above, in front of):
The butterfly is ______ the flower.
The worm is ______ the rock.
The ant is ______ the anthill.
The cricket is ______ two leaves.
The bee is flying ______ the flower.

grammar.title: Position Words: Use It
grammar.explanation: Position words tell us where something is. When we watch bugs, we need these words to say exactly where each one is — on a leaf, under a rock, or in a hole. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 6 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
on → touching the top:  “The butterfly is on the flower.”
under → below:  “The worm is under the rock.”
in → inside:  “The ant is in the anthill.”
between → in the middle of two things:  “The cricket is between two leaves.”
above → higher than:  “The bee is above the flower.”
in front of → just before:  “The spider is in front of its web.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “The ant is on the anthill.” (when it is inside)  ✗
Fix: If the ant is inside, we say in: “The ant is in the anthill.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Use on when something touches the top of a thing. Use in when something is inside a thing. A bug on a box sits on top; a bug in a box is inside it.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense for Facts
grammar.explanation: We use the simple present tense to talk about facts — things that are always true. This is perfect for telling facts about bugs, like what they have and what they do.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with: I, you, we, they, and plural nouns (more than one).
Insects have six legs.
Bees make honey.
Spiders spin webs.
Add -s (or -es) to the verb with: he, she, it, and one thing (singular).
A butterfly has colourful wings.
A cricket makes a chirping sound.
A spider spins a web.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “A spider spin a web.”  ✗    Fix: One spider, so add “s”: “A spider spins a web.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One bug → add the “s” to the verb. Many bugs → no “s”. Think: “One bee buzzes; two bees buzz.”
grammar.practice:
Write the correct form of the verb in brackets:
Butterflies ______ (fly) from flower to flower.
A spider ______ (spin) a web to catch insects.
Ants ______ (live) in colonies.
A cricket ______ (make) sounds with its wings.
Worms ______ (help) make the soil healthy.

grammar.title: Simple Present Tense for Facts: Use It
grammar.explanation: We use the simple present tense to talk about facts — things that are always true. This is perfect for telling facts about bugs, like what they have and what they do. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 6 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use the plain verb with: I, you, we, they, and plural nouns (more than one).
Insects have six legs.
Bees make honey.
Spiders spin webs.
Add -s (or -es) to the verb with: he, she, it, and one thing (singular).
A butterfly has colourful wings.
A cricket makes a chirping sound.
A spider spins a web.
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “A spider spin a web.”  ✗    Fix: One spider, so add “s”: “A spider spins a web.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: One bug → add the “s” to the verb. Many bugs → no “s”. Think: “One bee buzzes; two bees buzz.”
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

grammar.title: Asking Questions (What, Where, How)
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions! When we want to learn about bugs, we use question words. Each one asks for a different kind of answer.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use What to ask about a thing, Where to ask about a place, and How to ask about a way of doing something.
What do ants eat?  →  “They eat leaves and seeds.”
Where do spiders live?  →  “They live in webs.”
How do bees help people?  →  “They make honey.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “Where do bees help people?”  ✗   Fix: We are asking about a way, so we use How: “How do bees help people?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: What = a thing.   Where = a place.   How = a way.  Match the question word to the kind of answer you want.
grammar.practice:
Write What, Where or How to start each question:
______ do crickets make their sound?  →  “By rubbing their wings.”
______ do ants live?  →  “In an anthill under the ground.”
______ do butterflies eat?  →  “Nectar from flowers.”

grammar.title: Asking Questions (What, Where, How): Use It
grammar.explanation: Good learners ask questions! When we want to learn about bugs, we use question words. Each one asks for a different kind of answer. Now apply the same language independently in the Unit 6 context.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use What to ask about a thing, Where to ask about a place, and How to ask about a way of doing something.
What do ants eat?  →  “They eat leaves and seeds.”
Where do spiders live?  →  “They live in webs.”
How do bees help people?  →  “They make honey.”
grammar.commonMistake: Mistake: “Where do bees help people?”  ✗   Fix: We are asking about a way, so we use How: “How do bees help people?”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: What = a thing.   Where = a place.   How = a way.  Match the question word to the kind of answer you want.
grammar.practice: Create four new examples that follow this rule. Read each one aloud, check it against the model, and correct one possible mistake.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Name and Describe a Bug
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose a bug. Get ready to say:
“This is a ______.”
“It has ______ legs.”
“It lives ______.”
“It can ______.”
Record yourself. Check: Did you name the bug correctly? Did you say the right number of legs? Did you speak clearly?

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Where Is the Bug?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at a picture of a garden. Use a position word to say where each bug is:
“The butterfly is on the flower.”
“The ant is under the leaf.”
“The cricket is between two rocks.”

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Bug Riddles
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
Choose a bug, but do not say its name.
Give three clues: “It has six legs. It can fly. It makes honey.”
Ask: “What bug am I thinking of?” Then let the other person guess and swap turns.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Bug Scientist
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Describe one insect using its body parts, movement and habitat.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Include six legs, three body parts and antennae when they are correct.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Where Is the Bug?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Place or imagine six bugs and describe each position for a partner to find.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use on, under, in, between, above and in front of.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - Bug Question Circle
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask and answer two What, two Where and two How questions about bugs.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Use complete questions and factual answers.
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 1 — Describe a Bug
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Choose one bug. Write three or four sentences about it. Use these ideas to help you:
What is it called?
How many legs does it have?
What colour is it?
Where does it live?  What can it do?
Example: A butterfly is a colourful insect. It has six legs and two wings. Butterflies fly from flower to flower. They drink nectar.
writing.modelText: A butterfly has six legs and two wings. It rests on a flower. I can observe it without touching it.
writing.sentenceStarter: A butterfly has six legs and two wings.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Where Do Bugs Live?
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write six sentences using a different position word in each one:
The ______ is on the ______.
The ______ is under the ______.
The ______ is in the ______.
The ______ is between the ______.
The ______ is above the ______.
The ______ is in front of the ______.
writing.modelText: A butterfly has six legs and two wings. It rests on a flower. I can observe it without touching it.
writing.sentenceStarter: A butterfly has six legs and two wings.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Story response: Samira and the Little Garden Friends
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write three sentences about a bug you have seen. Start with: “I saw a ______. It ______.”
Draw Samira's garden with a butterfly, a bee, an ant, a spider and a worm. Under your picture, write one sentence saying where each one is, using a position word.
writing.modelText: Samira was nervous about bugs at first. She watched a butterfly, a bee, ants and a spider carefully. She learned to respect small garden animals.
writing.sentenceStarter: Samira was nervous about bugs at first.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; used capital letters, spaces and end punctuation; reread my work.
writing.support: Use the sentence starter, word bank and model. Say each sentence aloud before writing.
writing.extension: Add two precise details, a joining word and one sentence explaining why.

writing.title: Writing 4 - My Insect Fact Card
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six facts about one insect, including body parts, habitat, movement and food.
writing.modelText: A bee is an insect. It has six legs, three body parts and two antennae. Bees collect nectar from flowers.
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 5 - Insect or Not?
writing.promptAndInstructions: Compare an insect with a spider or worm and explain why only one is an insect.
writing.modelText: An ant is an insect because it has six legs and three body parts. A spider is not an insect because it has eight legs.
writing.sentenceStarter: An ant is an insect because it has six legs and three body parts
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation
writing.support: Use the model, Unit word bank and sentence starters. Say each sentence before writing it.
writing.extension: Add one precise detail and explain why it matters.
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

writing.title: Writing 6 - A Tiny Garden Story
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence story about finding and safely observing a small garden animal.
writing.modelText: I found a cricket under a leaf. I watched quietly and did not touch its home.
writing.sentenceStarter: I found a cricket under a leaf
writing.successCriteria: I answered every part; used Unit vocabulary; wrote complete sentences; checked capitals, spelling and punctuation

activities.title: Activity 1 — Fill in the Blanks (Bug Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the missing word on each line.
All insects have ______ legs.   Answer: ______
Spiders have ______ legs, so they are not insects.   Answer: ______
Insects have ______ main body parts.   Answer: ______
Bees collect ______ from flowers to make honey.   Answer: ______
Ants live together in groups called ______.   Answer: ______

activities.title: Activity 2 — True or False (Insect Facts)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
All insects have eight legs.
Butterflies are insects.
Spiders are insects.
Worms are not insects.
activities.answerSummary: 1. False (insects have six legs)  2. True  3. False (spiders have eight legs)  4. True

activities.title: Activity 3 — Fill in the Position Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with a position word (on, under, in, between, above, in front of).
The butterfly is ______ the flower.   Answer: ______
The worm is ______ the rock.   Answer: ______
The ant is ______ the anthill.   Answer: ______
The cricket is ______ two leaves.   Answer: ______

activities.title: Activity 4 - Build an Insect
activities.instructionsAndItems: Label the head, thorax, abdomen, six legs and antennae on an insect drawing.

assignments.title: All About Bugs learning portfolio

quizzes.question: What does 'butterfly' mean?
quizzes.options: A flying insect that collects nectar and makes honey. | A small insect that lives in large groups and works together. | A small jumping insect that makes a chirping sound. | An insect with large, colourful wings that flies from flower to flower.
quizzes.correctAnswer: An insect with large, colourful wings that flies from flower to flower.
quizzes.explanation: The butterfly landed on the red flower to drink nectar.

quizzes.question: Which word means: A small jumping insect that makes a chirping sound.
quizzes.explanation: We heard a cricket chirping in the garden at night.

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'ant'?
quizzes.explanation: The ants carried food back to their anthill.

quizzes.question: The ____ buzzed around the flowers collecting nectar.
quizzes.explanation: The bee buzzed around the flowers collecting nectar.

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The spider built a web between two branches.

quizzes.question: Which word means: A small, long animal with no legs that lives in the soil.
quizzes.explanation: worm: A small, long animal with no legs that lives in the soil. After the rain, we saw worms on the ground.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which word means: A small animal with six legs, a body in three parts, and usually wings.
quizzes.explanation: insect: A small animal with six legs, a body in three parts, and usually wings. A butterfly is an insect with beautiful wings.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: What type of word is 'legs'?
quizzes.explanation: legs: The body parts that animals use for walking or standing. Insects have six legs, but spiders have eight legs.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Butterflies have colourful ____, but ants do not always have wings.
quizzes.explanation: wings: The body parts that some insects and birds use for flying. Butterflies have colourful wings, but ants do not always have wings.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

quizzes.question: Which spelling is correct?
quizzes.explanation: antennae: The thin, movable parts on an insect's head that help it feel and smell. The butterfly's antennae help it find flowers.
quizzes.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 scalable conversion

teacherNotes.note:
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the text about insects with me and explain the hard words?” “What does ‘chirping’ mean?” “Can you play a listening game about bugs?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you help me practise naming and describing bugs?” “Am I saying these bug names correctly?” “Can you guess my bug riddle?”
Lesson: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check if my bug description is correct?” “What other details can I add to make my writing more interesting?”
Story: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words?” “Can you check my sentences about the bug I saw?” “Can you ask me questions about the story to see if I understood?”
Grammar: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “Can you check my grammar answers?” “When do I add -s to a verb for facts?” “Can you give me five more sentences to practise position words?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “What is the difference between a bug and an insect?” “Can you show me pictures of these garden animals?” “What other bugs are common in East Africa?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “How can I tell if a small animal is an insect?” “Why is a spider not an insect?” “What are the three body parts of an insect called?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “When do I use ‘on’ and when do I use ‘in’?” “Can you check if my position word sentences are correct?”
Vocabulary: 💬 Ask Your AI Tutor “How do I use these action words in sentences?” “What other verbs can I use to talk about what bugs do?”

Term placement approved. Outcome mappings, comprehension guidance, vocabulary enrichment, assessments, live sessions, rubrics, writing specifications and answer-key coverage completed in the approved reference model.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: 1. six   2. eight   3. three   4. nectar   5. colonies
1. False (insects have six legs)  2. True  3. False (spiders have eight legs)  4. True
1. on   2. under   3. in   4. between
1. on   2. under   3. in   4. between   5. above
1. fly   2. spins   3. live   4. makes   5. help
1. How   2. Where   3. What

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: All insects have six legs. They have three main body parts: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Antennae help insects feel and smell things around them.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Spiders are not insects, because they have eight legs. Worms are not insects, because they have no legs at all.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Where did it go?
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Where did it go?

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Where did it go?
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Where did it go?

Answers will vary. Accept a clear, relevant response in a complete sentence with a reason, example or text detail where appropriate.
Marking guidance: Award full credit when the learner answers every part of the prompt and supports the response with a sensible detail.

A cricket was in Grandpa's pocket, and he found it in the garden.
Marking guidance: Award full credit for this answer or an equivalent response that communicates the same meaning.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” — “It is a cricket.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Grandpa, what is that in your pocket?” — “It is a cricket.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Crickets need to stay warm.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Crickets need to stay warm.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Her older brother Ishmael was already there, sitting very still on a low rock.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Her older brother Ishmael was already there, sitting very still on a low rock.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “Is the bee going to sting me?” Samira asked, pulling back.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “Is the bee going to sting me?” Samira asked, pulling back.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “A spider is not an insect,” said Ishmael.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “A spider is not an insect,” said Ishmael.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house. Samira loved the garden.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: Early one warm morning, Samira went out to the garden behind her house. Samira loved the garden.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: “When we understand something,” she said, “we stop being afraid of it.

Accept an answer that communicates this evidence: And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.” Samira looked out at the dark garden, where all her little friends were resting.
Marking guidance: The response should point to this evidence from the text: And when we care for even the smallest creature, our hearts grow bigger.” Samira looked out at the dark garden, where all her little friends were resting.
Correct answer: An insect with large, colourful wings that flies from flower to flower.. The butterfly landed on the red flower to drink nectar.
Correct answer: cricket. We heard a cricket chirping in the garden at night.
Correct answer: Noun. The ants carried food back to their anthill.
Correct answer: bee. The bee buzzed around the flowers collecting nectar.
Correct answer: spider. The spider built a web between two branches.
Assess the writing with rub-writing-v1 and the recording with rub-speaking-v1. Award up to 16 marks for each artifact.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: insect. insect: A small animal with six legs, a body in three parts, and usually wings. A butterfly is an insect with beautiful wings.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Noun. legs: The body parts that animals use for walking or standing. Insects have six legs, but spiders have eight legs.
